body:
### Problem

I was asked by https://github.com/folke/lazy.nvim/discussions/2139 to create a new issue because creating plugin managers is a recurring topic. So trying to sum up the many universes which exist and what you eventually can learn from them and what my conclusion kinda is -> data more important than the implementation. Maybe a neov-plugin-manager used by neovim would turn into another killer project cause one team one case never fits them all. Just red and enjoy the diversity of todays world ..

Who am I ?
I was hooked by Nixpkgs (maybe blindly). It solves the distribution issue.
I love cmake (it solves the developer problem) - but how to turn cmake into nix ?
I love buck2/bazel -> cause they kinda do what El Dolstra (author of nixpkgs/nixos)
envisioned: Merging make rules and package management ! Like big dependency graph.
Maybe PCs that time weren't strong enough when nix* started.
That's why I created vim-addon-manager because I wanted to share my own code
which had >common library functions< like others (tpope, tlib) I mots of you
might have heard of it.
VAM solved
- lazy activation
- integration into nix
- dependency language (foucsing on latest to keep it simple => flaw)
introduced vim-pi (package database) so that all old viml scripts had an name
you could install because human brains worked best with names .

Community decided to support 3+ managers pollutting all README.md files on github
If you use vam then else then else then else then else then .. you get.
AI does help .. but is not perfect.

I enjoyed rading about https://github.com/neovim/packspec
which is basically same intention as addon-info.json had (wild west ..)

From Java its known that mysql-2.0 and mysql-3.0 can't be loaded at the same
time without massive hacks. Incompatible version maybe should be within the name ?
Then you can import mysql2 or mysql3. Eg Python Zarr libarry caused a huge mess
moving from 2 to 3 with 3 having some compatibility layer more readonly.
and import zarr never told you what you actually worke with (import zarr2 or
import zarr3 would)..

But the real issue is that you can't get it right.
Nixos/Nixpkgs honestly while its nice I used it its hard to maintain 5+ mysql
versions cause you have to do it manually. Docker just works everywhere.
So for many projects I ditched nixos using docker compose.

In the past Vim challenges
- PHP fork
- Python
- Perl
- Ruby ? I forgot
=> many might know YMC (cpp dependencies)

Nvim:
- introduced protocol
- viml
- lua

And tools like krita, Inkscape, Gimp, Blender, Qgis, .. all have at least Python
dependencies.

Tools like digikam can run on multiple OS. But how to ensure you use same
version on OSX/Linux/Mariadb to use your photos from external disk?

JS/Rust/ .. like all kinda suck on nixpkgs if they require C dependencies to
build native code (partially solved by Webassembly)

So potential extermes / learnings:

  - big system like nixos means you have to mantain the many realtionships you
    don't care about.

  - cmake like do it yourself can't be packaged easily by nix.
    Because it requires understanding cmake fetching dependencies and packaging
    them, too

  - buck2 fixes some ABI so only runs on most linux (but honestly Windows ?).

  - xmake like systems have their own >what they needed< dependency systems
    which kinda interface with other managers (eg C dependency managers) as
    needed to get a goal running. Cool project But when I tried even compiling X
    like failed - ok its understaffed I guess yet another interesting way.

    but its imperative, not deterministic

  - Haskell/cabal (like darcs) learned that too many options (like linux :)
    is a problem, too. So they created platforms (6+ native packages bundled
    with GHC) something like >one windows< you know what to target ..

  - many Haskell packages maintainers didn't pump the versions but code was
    working fine with newer GHCs ..

  - even with PIP you can't reproduce because newer PIP resolves other versions
    -> see pip-python-packages.from(2020-10-06) proposal below


So the perfect tool kinda allows you to do what you want:

  - platforms (to keep overall overhead low)

  - define pools (ubuntu native packages) augmented with my own selection
    I want to make work -> for hacking or get this thing done

  - knows many languages because there are dependencies like mysql C extensions
    for the many languages or openxr or vulkan or gl or ..

What has happened SAT solving libsolve (suse) kinda took over many eco systems.
yet SAT takes much time and the darcss probem (you can change order of patches
but you don't want to cause you're hitting the millions of possibilities you can't cope with ..)

So today I think a solution should look like this:

 pools = [ native-ubuntu, .. my-stuff, pip-python-packages.from(2020-10-06)]

 solved = solver(pools, patches) < allows patching !

  -> YEAH .. wishful thinking *LOL*.
 build_env = [
  solved.nvim
  solved.python.pyright
  solved.python.xx
  solved.krita.xx
  solved.blender.xx
  solved.blender.xx
 ]

Now about plugin management. Like GPU (julia vs AOT) there are many ways to
think about it and all make sense.

- lazy downloading (cause not all users want) preservi disk space (want lean system)
- ahead of time downloading (type checking, want to work offline)
- be fast (lapce) vs lazy (vscode) both work .. but honestly JS happened to turn out
  to be a nice dev experience cause only the parts which are used get jitted.
- ..

So I think a NeoVim specific packaging system must be seen like this:
externalize a solver (which knowns where to put stuff) which allows 
ubuntu and nixpkgs deployments. Which asbtracts given that pool resolve
dependency = "XXX" by name.

THUS:
user defines pool
user defines solver
user defines installation-engine

given that information neovim nvim.lazy vam .. all get unemployed.
all which is required (either lazy loading, AOT , ..)
ask the configuration to install and tell when its ready.

Because then you can add constraints like krita / neovim sharing same python
version.

About lazy.nvim I like that it moved the mapping .. configurations nearby the plugin.
(which is why I backported it to vim-addon-manager). Yet having defaults might
make sense.

VIM had one issue: There was a forum. But no good leadership about user plugins.
So I wrote VAM to solve an issue. It did :) but it wasn't picked up.
Talking to alterantive solutions (Japan I forgot) I later noticed other people
just didn't know about it.

And one of the big problems of the internet is -> reinvention of the wheel.
But communitise strive if they don't (unless there is a good reason).

Whether programming languages divide the communities way too much is yet
another hot topic I care about deeply but don't have funding to fix.
Cause neovim vs lapce why do both solutoins exist ? One is C+Lua the other is Rust.
But Rust has 2+ scripting languagse and it looks like they settle on GC more and more
and there are Rust forks with GC (which don't run on OSX maybe they do now ..)
Why can't you write shell scripts with Java - cause you don't have shell
completion cause every args is its own langage depending on the app.
once that was fixed you could use java as shell.
-> but that's a different story ping me if you feel the same ..

So I want to remind that Neovim *IS* the editor I use every day.
Yet its part of bigger changing and varying eco systems and package managers.

Eg JS on debian is many times slower than pnpm. They tried packaging them all
but  you can't get it right.

Emacs and dependencies (Xemacs,emacs, .. VAM like .. ), builtins , .. home
manager for nix you can't get it right.

So what's left is building a system which comes closer which understands its
part of a bigger world.

So to be honets I think Neovim maintainers should think hard about whether they
want to build package manager *INSIDE* of neovim or join something bigger which
can be used by Neovim - or if no tinstalled could be implemnetd by Neovim.

But getting the data (package databases right) could be the more important piece.

And the rest will fall into place for all solutions:
- Emacs 5x managers
- Vim-like 5x managers
- Nix and the scheme fork
- gentoo
- debian
- chocolately like
- brew
- pip and the many language specific ones (important due to language-servers)
- buck2/bazel
- docker

And even PIP (pip env, micromamba, conda, ..) has 4+ ways to install packages today.

Neovim itself could be the manager or part of the universe and should work both
ways.

And the future might be /bin/(await neovim) and it starts. Like Fuchsia (Google).
They load data from the internet and data can be code and libraries.
So that's going to be a totally different story so package management might actually
move to OS level in the future cause that manages the filesystem and maybe package managing
and hot patching (kernel) again .. similar goals .. the more AI the more bugs
the more critical bugs .. thus looking forward to be part of this future.

1) define package dependenices
2) allow different solutions to make sense out of the mess
3) I think neo-package-manager used by neo-vim lapce emacs etc *lol*

would be a cool future and maybe a classic fix point which would be that good that it might not get reinvented or replaced again. At least I can't think of something better. But one person has no chance achieving this without burning out :-)

And yes of course some sane folks of course embed Vim within Eclipse etc.
Don't forget those :-P
I wasn't even talking about bubblewrap like user envs nix-ld hacks etc . timelines show that eg pnpm/yarn/npm/deno/.. all kinda .. well some stuff gets backported. Thus if we manage to get the wild west dependency definition language / solver right we'd help a lot of eco systems I think. And maybe it looks liek this her is a meta package code which applied to a target system (mobile / watch / VR / ..) turns into a lean package description to be used by solver so that that is fast cause we all love fast software and scripting languages like lua, right ? :)


### Expected behavior

- it should harmonize with the many OS
- plugins with C dependencies should harmonize with users choices :)
- it should lead and not reinvent which means honoring brew, apt, nix, cmake, and the many more ?
